metadata.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: EDHOC and OSCORE ACE profile slides IETF 125

But something went wrong at the resource server that, for example, due to memory limitations, had to delete the old access token to supersede and terminates with that the token series and the secure communication association with the client. Because of this particular reason, the resource server replies with an error to DAS. DAS gives up with a new workflow and just replies to the client. Sorry, I tried, I failed. Here's the token, you can continue. We were aware of that already. And the question from Dave was, well, what next? Can you clarify? And we already added a paragraph clarifying explicitly what happens next. Well, the client will follow up using the token obtained from DAS. And the client will fail too, because from the client point of view, the secure communication association is still ongoing, although it's not on the resource server. So the resource server will reply with an error. The client now is really lost and can possibly ask for a brand new access token more, starting a new series at DAS. So what happens next is clear now, but can we do better than this? We think so. This is not in the draft yet. But we plan to include it in the next version. If that particular situation that I described, of course, yeah, we can do something better. Take a step back again on DAS failing to upload the access token at DRS, and DRS replying back to DAS with an error. Well, in that case, in that particular case, DAS can instead locally start a new token series issuing and the SHA can then react as DAS old original access token requests that triggered the token series of the latest access token for which the upload failed. So, the AS in practice starts a new token series with its first access token, TNEO2. But what then? Well, it depends. If the client made that original old access token request, also including the parameter 2RS with information to be conveyed to the resource server, well, then there's nothing the AS can do much more right now. And it really returns the latest issued access token TNEO2 to the client, and the client can take it from there, and now it should work for the client. But if in that old original access token request, the client didn't add that 2RS parameter with information, then the AS can do even better, and consistent with the requested use of the new workflow from the client, the AS can try to upload this TNEO2 access token to the 2RS, again using the new workflow. There's no risk of loops. There's no risk for the root of the problem situation to occur here, because this is the new first access token of the new token series. These are the steps that I imagine to happen, but what do we need to have in the draft beyond the steps themselves? Well, the AS needs to know from the resource server that exactly that situation occurs, and we can do that thanks to the error response that the resource server returns to the AS by defining simply new ACER error code to be included in the payload of that response. Now, this is interesting because ACER error codes were originally defined to be used in responses imagined from the authorization server to the client on the resource server, and here it would be in an error response from the RS to the AS instead, but there's fundamentally nothing forbidding the use of ACER error codes in different messages than those imagined originally. The AS also needs to remember the original access token request that started the terminated token series, but this is already happening. Otherwise, we couldn't enforce the dynamically updated access rights in the first place. And finally, when providing an access token response to the client with the new access token in there or not, depending on how the uploading is actually done, we have to tell the client what exactly has happened, meaning I know you asked for dynamically updating access rights, but actually that precise thing went wrong and I've just started a new token series. And here's the token or an indication that I successfully uploaded the access token to you. My name is Richard Hoeglund and I will be presenting updates to the addoc and OSCORE profile of ACE, which is now reached version 10. And just as an overview. What is this about? Well, it's a profile of the ACE framework using addoc to do the actual key establishment where the access token is uploaded in an EAD item. And then.    It's using OSCORE for the actual secure communication based on the key material derived through the addoc execution. You can see the workflow here. An example workflow where you start running addoc and. You can also see that in this example, we're using the optimization such that we're actually combining two of the OSCORE messages. And one thing to note, though, is that this can also be used. To be run in the reverse message flow. This is an example of the forward message flow. But basically, you request the access token. You post it. You execute addoc there in steps one and two on the right. In step number three. The first OSCORE request combined with the addoc message number three. And in the addoc message number three, the access token is included in an addoc EAD item. And to go through some of the updates. We did for version number ten. First of all, we removed a number of parameters from the addoc information object. And this was based on feedback from some previous ITF meetings and discussions. And just to mention that the addoc information object is providing information to the resource server and client on how to execute addoc with each other. For instance, what ciphers you use and similar details. However, some of the parameters in this object were excessive or a bit unclear in how useful they actually are. So we decided to remove four of these parameters about the maximum size of messages. Usage of the co-op content format option. Endpoint identifier types. And the actual transports use. And as a side effect of this, we could also remove two registries for the transport and the addoc endpoint identifier. We also did a number of editorial fixes and improvements and also some minor updates based on an IANA early review. Another thing we did is we fixed some CDL notation describing this addoc information object. And here you can see a better illustration of what it actually contains. So basically, we noticed that the CDL definition was not very good.  And we did not actually validate. It's now been updated to be correct. And if you see it on the left hand side, all the green boxes have been updated. And those four items in the red box have been removed. And well, basically, as you can see, there's been a number of updates. One main thing I would say is about the trust anchors where we simply had map before. But now we actually defined this. And in proper CDL. Another thing we did is we defined how the NS is derived when you're combining this profile with a specific application profiles of ACE. So what's the scenario? Well, you can use this endoconon score profile in combination with some ACE application profiles such as key group common score. And the ACE core pub sub profile. Now, the problem is that those profiles require the client to receive a challenge NS from the resource server. And then NS is in turn used by the client to perform proof of possession. Now, the problem is that in this profile that we're defining, the client doesn't actually upload the first access token for token series to the OCD info endpoint. As I said, instead, the access token is placed inside the OCD.   So it's actually uploaded during the execution of the ad hoc itself and not OCD info. And because of this, NS is never received by the client. So because of this, we propose a solution that when this profile is used, you actually derive NS by means of the ad hoc exporter. So basically, well, you can see that the actual details on how you do it.  You have a specific ad hoc exporter label and we propose the registration of two labels, 26 and 27 for the two application profiles. The key group common score and the core pub sub profile. You specify the context parameter and you specify the length parameter. And then you invoke the ad hoc exporter. As you can see there in the image, how it's defined. And during that, as output of that invocation, you get NS. And then you invoke the ad hoc exporter. And then, well, you obviously invoke this on the ad hoc session that the client and resource server are sharing. So another point we wanted to raise is to propose early allocation of some code points. We would like to do early allocation for three registries, actually. And this is also something that was raised through feedback from other ITF collaborators. The first registry is the CWTE confirmation methods. And you can see there a number of items would like to propose for early allocation. The second registry is the CWTE confirmation methods. And there you can see a number of items and our proposed integer values. And finally, we would like to do with for that of external authorization data registry. And here, we actually have one value, credential by value, which is already today in use by AIO co-op. And here, we actually have one value, credential by value, which is already today in use by AIO co-op. and Arial OS core. So this is something we would like to propose to do this whole allocation. So what's our next steps? We have a number of open points and try to highlight some of the more important ones. One thing we want to elaborate a bit more on is the privacy and security implications of including the access token in ED2, ED3 or ED4, because like I mentioned, we support both the forward and reverse message flow of EDOC. And depending on the flow, you would place the access token in, well, basically you can place the access token in different of the EDOC messages. So ED2 is message two, ED3 is message three and ED4 would be message four. So now depending on the flow and depending on where you are placing the access token, that can actually have results.     In terms of privacy and security. And we would like to have a better kind of overview of those implications. Another point we would like to do is a bit more extended example of usage of this request creation hints ED item. And that's basically a way for the client to ask for request creation hints and the RS to provide request creation hints through an ED, EDOC event.  So that's basically the idea. We also would like to process Christian Amsis review of version zero nine. Another point is that since the AS is producing this EDOC information object, it needs to have information that's accurate with regards to how the client and RS should execute EDOC. So it's important that the AS is kept up to date about the capabilities of C and RS and how they would successfully execute EDOC with each other. Thank you. Yeah. And that's just to mention any comments and reviews are very welcome. So thank you for listening. All right. Thank you. Any comments or questions? All right. In that case, thank you, everyone. It looks like we have one action item coming out of this.  Which is the working group last call for Co-op SOS core. Other than that, thank you, everyone. And enjoy the rest of your day. So we have someone coming off the mic. Oh, sorry. So Co-op SOS core is already in the working group last call. So this is working group last call. Thank you for the correction. We are finishing off. We are concluding the working group last call. And we will write it up. We will write the Shepard write up. Thank you. All right. In that case, you still have permission to have a nice rest of your day. So thank you, everyone. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.   Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.     Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

My name is Richard Hoeglund and I will be presenting updates to the addoc and OSCORE profile of ACE, which is now reached version 10.
And just as an overview.
What is this about?
Well, it's a profile of the ACE framework using addoc to do the actual key establishment where the access token is uploaded in an EAD item.
And then.
It's using OSCORE for the actual secure communication based on the key material derived through the addoc execution.
You can see the workflow here.
An example workflow where you start running addoc and.
You can also see that in this example, we're using the optimization such that we're actually combining two of the OSCORE messages.
And one thing to note, though, is that this can also be used.
To be run in the reverse message flow.
This is an example of the forward message flow.
But basically, you request the access token.
You post it.
You execute addoc there in steps one and two on the right.
In step number three.
The first OSCORE request combined with the addoc message number three.
And in the addoc message number three, the access token is included in an addoc EAD item.
And to go through some of the updates.
We did for version number ten.
First of all, we removed a number of parameters from the addoc information object.
And this was based on feedback from some previous ITF meetings and discussions.
And just to mention that the addoc information object is providing information to the resource server and client on how to execute addoc with each other.
For instance, what ciphers you use and similar details.
However, some of the parameters in this object were excessive or a bit unclear in how useful they actually are.
So we decided to remove four of these parameters about the maximum size of messages.
Usage of the co-op content format option.
Endpoint identifier types.
And the actual transports use.
And as a side effect of this, we could also remove two registries for the transport and the addoc endpoint identifier.
We also did a number of editorial fixes and improvements and also some minor updates based on an IANA early review.
Another thing we did is we fixed some CDL notation describing this addoc information object.
And here you can see a better illustration of what it actually contains.
So basically, we noticed that the CDL definition was not very good.
And we did not actually validate.
It's now been updated to be correct.
And if you see it on the left hand side, all the green boxes have been updated.
And those four items in the red box have been removed.
And well, basically, as you can see, there's been a number of updates.
One main thing I would say is about the trust anchors where we simply had map before.
But now we actually defined this.
And in proper CDL.
Another thing we did is we defined how the NS is derived when you're combining this profile with a specific application profiles of ACE.
So what's the scenario?
Well, you can use this endoconon score profile in combination with some ACE application profiles such as key group common score.
And the ACE core pub sub profile.
Now, the problem is that those profiles require the client to receive a challenge NS from the resource server.
And then NS is in turn used by the client to perform proof of possession.
Now, the problem is that in this profile that we're defining, the client doesn't actually upload the first access token for token series to the OCD info endpoint.
As I said, instead, the access token is placed inside the OCD.
So it's actually uploaded during the execution of the ad hoc itself and not OCD info.
And because of this, NS is never received by the client.
So because of this, we propose a solution that when this profile is used, you actually derive NS by means of the ad hoc exporter.
So basically, well, you can see that the actual details on how you do it.
You have a specific ad hoc exporter label and we propose the registration of two labels, 26 and 27 for the two application profiles.
The key group common score and the core pub sub profile.
You specify the context parameter and you specify the length parameter.
And then you invoke the ad hoc exporter.
As you can see there in the image, how it's defined.
And during that, as output of that invocation, you get NS.
And then you invoke the ad hoc exporter.
And then, well, you obviously invoke this on the ad hoc session that the client and resource server are sharing.
So another point we wanted to raise is to propose early allocation of some code points.
We would like to do early allocation for three registries, actually.
And this is also something that was raised through feedback from other ITF collaborators.
The first registry is the
CWTE confirmation methods.
And you can see there a number of items would like to propose for early allocation.
The second registry is the
CWTE confirmation methods.
And there you can see a number of items and our proposed integer values.
And finally, we would like to do with for that of external authorization data registry.
And here, we actually have one value, credential by value, which is already today in use by AIO co-op.
And here, we actually have one value, credential by value, which is already today in use by AIO co-op.
and Arial OS core.

One thing we want to elaborate a bit more on is the privacy and security implications
of including the access token in ED2, ED3 or ED4, because like I mentioned, we support
both the forward and reverse message flow of EDOC.
And depending on the flow, you would place the access token in, well, basically you can
place the access token in different of the EDOC messages.
So ED2 is message two, ED3 is message three and ED4 would be message four.
So now depending on the flow and depending on where you are placing the access token,
that can actually have results.
In terms of privacy and security.
And we would like to have a better kind of overview of those implications.
Another point we would like to do is a bit more extended example of usage of this request
creation hints ED item.
